svg.font-family: -apple-system, BlinkMacSystemFont, 'Segoe UI', Roboto, 'Helvetica Neue', Arial, sans-serif
svg.text: Three specialist agents, each a decision-maker at one or more gates. Agents return decisions (not just outputs); the orchestrator respects them.
GUARDRAIL HOOKS + POLICY INVARIANTS (cross-cutting, enforced in code, not negotiable by agents)
PreIngest: PII/PHI/secrets redaction • PostAgent: schema + citation + confidence validation • PreDeliver: quiet hours, rate limits, approved channels, dedupe • Audit: every decision + reason logged

g.text: Signals: visible behavior change • customer-facing surface •
workflow change • integration impact • support load •
If ARCHIVE → stop. If ESCALATE → HITL. If COMMUNICATE → continue to gate 2.
Signals: imminent rollout • decision window open •
already shipped • docs ready • early flag stage (hold) •
If HOLD → re-queued with re-eval trigger. If RIPE → continue to gate 3.
GATE 3 — Clear enough to hand off?
Signals: concrete before/after • named impact areas •
If NEED_CLARIFICATION or UNRESOLVABLE → HITL. If READY → handoff.
GATE 4 — Is this BU actually affected?
Signals: touches BU workflow • alters BU output •
requires BU preparation • creates BU decision •
visible rollout in BU user base • BU owns area
NOT_AFFECTED → drop. AFFECTED → continue to gate 5.
action with owner • length matches weight •
Sequences agent invocations • respects agent decision verbs (ARCHIVE, HOLD, ESCALATE, etc.) • manages HITL queue • owns state machine • writes audit • no LLM reasoning at this layer
Agent decisions (ARCHIVE, HOLD_UNTIL, NOT_AFFECTED, NOT_WORTH, etc.) map to terminal or re-queued states
/replay <change-id> · /pending · /digest · /audit <date-range> · /metrics · /explain <change-id> (shows full decision chain from all gates)
All commands write to the audit log. /explain is the key operator tool for understanding why an agent made a decision.
WHY THIS IS A REAL AGENT TEAM
• Each agent is a decision-maker, not a task-executor. Agents return verbs (COMMUNICATE, HOLD, NOT_AFFECTED, WORTH_SENDING,
SEND_NOW, ESCALATE, …) that the orchestrator respects. The agents decide when to hand off and when to stop.
• Six judgment gates distributed across the three agents; each gate has explicit signals, decision verbs, and failure modes.
• Orchestrator is code — it manages state, respects agent decisions, enforces policy invariants, handles HITL.
• Policy invariants (quiet hours, rate limits, PII, approved channels) are enforced in code — not negotiable by agent reasoning.
• Every decision is logged with its reason. /explain shows the full decision chain for any change event.
• BUAtlas runs in parallel — one isolated invocation per candidate BU — preventing cross-BU context contamination.
• Decision criteria live in a separate document and drive all three agent prompts; updating criteria updates the prompts.
HOW THIS DIFFERS FROM A PIPELINE OF AGENTS
Pipeline: "call agent A, then B, then C, then send." Agents do tasks.
This: "Agent A decides whether there's anything to communicate, and if yes, whether it's ripe, and if yes, whether it's
clear enough to hand off. Agent B decides whether each BU is actually affected, and if yes, whether the draft is
worth that recipient's attention. Agent C decides whether now is the right moment to send." Agents exercise judgment.
Any gate can return a stop verb (ARCHIVE, HOLD_INDEFINITE, NOT_AFFECTED, NOT_WORTH) and the work ends there.
Any gate can return an escalate verb and a human takes over. Any gate can return a defer verb and the work re-queues.
The default bias is not to send unless concrete signals argue for sending. Recipient attention is the scarce resource.
